sourcePatterns.description: SMS OTP verification code input UI with auto-read capability — MFA interception page
sourcePatterns.note: Using the Web OTP API (OTPCredential) or autocomplete='one-time-code' with navigator.credentials.get to auto-read SMS verification codes is used by phishing pages to silently intercept MFA codes sent to the victim's phone.

sourcePatterns.description: Chain of single-digit OTP input fields with auto-focus — SMS code interception UI
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript that auto-advances focus between single-digit input fields with OTP/code/verify context creates the 6-digit verification code entry UI. This auto-focus chain combined with verification text is a phishing MFA intercept indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form only visible at mobile breakpoints — desktop hides credential harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: CSS media queries that hide login forms on desktop breakpoints (min-width >= 768px) while showing them on mobile create mobile-only phishing pages. Desktop-based security scanners see no form at all.

sourcePatterns.description: Camera/microphone permission requested on phishing page — surveillance or document capture
sourcePatterns.note: Requesting camera/microphone access on a credential harvesting page is used for selfie-based verification scams (fake KYC/ID verification) or to record the victim entering credentials. Legitimate login pages don't request camera access.

sourcePatterns.description: NFC/contactless payment API interaction on phishing page — payment data interception
sourcePatterns.note: Interacting with NFC/NDEF APIs to read payment card data or write malicious payment tags is a mobile-specific credential harvesting technique targeting tap-to-pay functionality.

sourcePatterns.description: Fine GPS location harvested alongside credentials — precise victim location tracking
sourcePatterns.note: Requesting fine GPS location alongside credential capture gives attackers the victim's exact physical location. This data enables physical attacks (home break-in, stalking) or verifies victim identity for targeted fraud.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript bridge to native app WebView detected — credential interception in app context
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript bridges to native WebView contexts (webkit.messageHandlers for iOS, AndroidBridge for Android) can intercept credentials entered in app-embedded browsers. Phishing pages detect they're in a WebView and use the bridge to exfiltrate data.

sourcePatterns.description: Fetch POST to Telegram API with credential data in body — direct exfiltration call
sourcePatterns.note: A fetch() POST to any URL containing 'telegram' with credential-related fields in the body is a direct Telegram exfiltration call. This catches both plain and partially obfuscated API URLs.

sourcePatterns.description: XMLHttpRequest to Telegram API — legacy exfiltration method still widely used
sourcePatterns.note: XMLHttpRequest-based Telegram API calls are used by older phishing kits and infostealers. While fetch() is more common in modern kits, many still use XHR for compatibility with older browsers.

sourcePatterns.description: Multipart form data constructed for Telegram file upload exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Constructing a FormData object with file/document/photo fields for Telegram API upload is the file exfiltration preparation pattern. The FormData is sent to sendDocument or sendPhoto endpoints.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook embed with credential field names — structured exfiltration report
sourcePatterns.note: Discord webhook embeds with fields containing credential labels create formatted exfiltration reports. Each embed field displays a stolen credential (email, password, IP, etc.) in a structured Discord message.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook POST with file attachment — exfiltrating credential files via Discord
sourcePatterns.note: Sending files via Discord webhooks using multipart FormData with payload_json is used to exfiltrate credential archives, screenshots, and cookie databases. Discord allows 8MB file attachments per webhook message.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram Bot API sendLocation — victim GPS coordinates exfiltrated to attacker
sourcePatterns.note: The sendLocation API endpoint sends GPS coordinates to a Telegram chat. Phishing kits that harvest geolocation data use this to send the victim's exact location to the attacker alongside credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook with multiple embeds containing different credential categories
sourcePatterns.note: Multiple Discord embeds in a single webhook call, each containing different credential categories (login info, payment data, personal info), create a comprehensive victim profile report sent as a single formatted message.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook simple content message with credential data — plaintext exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Simple Discord webhook messages with plaintext content containing 'email:', 'password:', etc. are basic credential exfiltration. Not all phishkits use embeds — many just send a plain text message with credential key:value pairs.

sourcePatterns.description: Form with absolute URL action and any text+password input combo — keyword-free credential harvest detection
sourcePatterns.note: Detects credential forms by STRUCTURE not keywords: a POST form with absolute cross-origin action containing both a text/email input and a password input. Catches phish even when all field names/IDs are randomized.

sourcePatterns.description: Page with 2+ password input fields — multi-password harvesting or password change phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Two password fields on the same page is unusual for legitimate login (which has one). It indicates either a 'current + new password' phishing page or a double-entry verification trick. Doesn't rely on field names.

sourcePatterns.description: Input field .value read followed by HTTP POST within 1000 chars — keyword-free exfiltration behavior
sourcePatterns.note: Reading any input .value then making an external HTTP request within 1000 chars is the behavioral skeleton of credential exfiltration. Works regardless of field names — catches .value → fetch() even with randomized variable names.

sourcePatterns.description: getElementById followed by external POST — structural credential read-then-send pattern
sourcePatterns.note: The getElementById → .value → POST chain is the fundamental credential harvesting behavior regardless of what the element IDs are named. Catches keyword-avoidance evasion.

sourcePatterns.description: querySelector targeting input elements followed by value extraction — generic field harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Using querySelector('input[...]') to select form inputs by type/attribute rather than name, then extracting .value and sending externally. This behavioral pattern survives complete field name randomization. [Observation-level: querySelector + value — any form processing code]

sourcePatterns.description: Credentials sent to a Cloudflare Worker endpoint — proxy-based exfiltration hiding final destination
sourcePatterns.note: Sending credential data to a workers.dev or pages.dev endpoint uses Cloudflare as a proxy. The Worker forwards credentials to the actual C2 (Telegram, Discord, email, custom server). This defeats direct endpoint matching.

sourcePatterns.description: Input type changed at runtime from text to password or vice versa — evasion of type=password detection
sourcePatterns.note: Changing an input field's type from 'text' to 'password' at runtime defeats static HTML scanning for type=password. The field appears as a text input in the source but becomes a password field when JavaScript executes.

sourcePatterns.description: Input field name/id set dynamically at runtime — avoids static field name matching
sourcePatterns.note: Setting input field names/IDs dynamically using decoded strings avoids static source analysis of field names. The decoded name might be 'password' but it never appears as a literal string in the source. [Observation-level: Runtime field name setting — React forms, dynamic form builders]

sourcePatterns.description: Form with no action attribute but JS submit handler — action set dynamically to avoid static analysis
sourcePatterns.note: A form with password input but NO action attribute, followed immediately by a script tag, means the form submission is entirely controlled by JavaScript. The exfil destination is set dynamically and never appears in the HTML.

sourcePatterns.description: Minimal HTML page with only a form and few elements — stripped-down phishing with no JS
sourcePatterns.note: A minimal HTML page (DOCTYPE → body → form → password → /body with little else) is a stripped-down phishing page with no JavaScript at all. All logic is server-side, making client-side detection extremely difficult. The minimalism itself is the signal.

sourcePatterns.description: All form inputs collected into object/array on submit — bulk data harvesting regardless of field names
sourcePatterns.note: Iterating over ALL form inputs and collecting their values into a data structure is a bulk harvesting technique that captures everything regardless of field names. The kit doesn't need to know field names — it just grabs all input values.

sourcePatterns.description: FormData constructed from entire form element — captures all fields without knowing names
sourcePatterns.note: new FormData(formElement) serializes ALL fields in a form automatically — the kit doesn't need to reference individual field names. This keyword-independent harvesting captures everything including hidden fields and CSRF tokens.

sourcePatterns.description: MutationObserver watching for input value changes — passive credential monitoring without event handlers
sourcePatterns.note: Using MutationObserver to passively watch for input value changes avoids attaching suspicious event listeners to form fields. The observer silently captures everything typed without any addEventListener or onkeypress.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript Proxy handler trapping property access — intercepts credential reads transparently
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript Proxy objects with get/set traps can transparently intercept any property access on form elements. When the legitimate page code reads input.value, the Proxy intercepts it and exfiltrates the value before returning it.

sourcePatterns.description: Event listener attached to all input elements via querySelectorAll — bulk keystroke monitoring
sourcePatterns.note: Attaching event listeners to ALL input elements via querySelectorAll('input').forEach(addEventListener) is bulk credential monitoring. It captures everything typed in any field without needing to know specific field selectors. [Observation-level: Listener on all inputs — form validation libraries (Formik, React Hook Form)]

sourcePatterns.description: Data exfiltrated via dynamically set CSS background-image URL — non-network-request exfil
sourcePatterns.note: Setting a CSS background-image URL that includes credential data exfiltrates via an image request that doesn't look like a fetch/XHR call. The browser loads the 'image' which is actually a tracking pixel URL containing the stolen data.

sourcePatterns.description: Image src dynamically set with credential data in URL — pixel-based exfiltration bypass
sourcePatterns.note: Creating an Image element and setting its src to a URL that includes form input .value data exfiltrates via an image request. This bypass technique doesn't use fetch/XHR/sendBeacon — the browser simply tries to 'load' the image containing the stolen data in the URL.

sourcePatterns.description: cf-turnstile-response token submitted alongside credential fields — Turnstile-gated phishing exfil
sourcePatterns.note: The cf-turnstile-response token being collected and submitted alongside credential field values confirms that Turnstile is being used to gate a credential harvesting form. The token proves the victim is human; the credentials are the payload.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile widget protecting a form with cross-origin action URL — credential exfil behind CAPTCHA gate
sourcePatterns.note: A Turnstile widget on the same page as a form with a cross-origin action URL containing a password field is almost certainly phishing. Legitimate sites that use Turnstile submit to their own domain, not to an external endpoint.

sourcePatterns.description: Turnstile token extracted and sent to external server alongside credentials — AiTM token relay
sourcePatterns.note: Extracting the Turnstile token and sending it to an external server alongside credentials is the AiTM relay pattern. The attacker uses the victim's solved Turnstile token to bypass CAPTCHA on the real site while stealing credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Hidden input named cf-turnstile-response alongside credential inputs — Turnstile token in exfil payload
sourcePatterns.note: A hidden input named cf-turnstile-response on the same form as a password field confirms Turnstile is gating a credential harvesting form. The token is submitted alongside the stolen credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft ESTSAUTH or ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT cookie captured in JavaScript — session token theft
sourcePatterns.note: ESTSAUTH and ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT are Microsoft's Entra ID session cookies. Finding these cookie names in JavaScript on a non-Microsoft domain means a phishing page is capturing Microsoft session tokens for replay. This is the core AiTM session hijacking indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: document.cookie read and sent to external endpoint — bulk session cookie exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Reading document.cookie and sending it externally via any channel (fetch, XHR, sendBeacon, postMessage, Image, WebSocket) is the fundamental AiTM session cookie exfiltration pattern. The stolen cookies allow session replay without re-authentication.

sourcePatterns.description: Specific auth cookie names extracted from document.cookie — targeted session token theft
sourcePatterns.note: AiTM cookie extraction targeting session tokens + exfil. Requires fetch/XHR/beacon/postMessage after extraction — without exfil, it is just cookie reading.

sourcePatterns.description: Set-Cookie values parsed from response headers and forwarded — proxy cookie relay in JavaScript
sourcePatterns.note: Parsing Set-Cookie values from response headers and forwarding them via JavaScript is the AiTM cookie relay pattern. The proxy captures session cookies from the real server's response and sends them to the attacker.

sourcePatterns.description: OAuth authorization code captured from URL and forwarded — OAuth flow interception
sourcePatterns.note: Extracting OAuth authorization codes, access tokens, or ID tokens from the URL and forwarding them to an external endpoint is the OAuth flow interception pattern used by indirect AiTM proxies (Storm-1167 style).

sourcePatterns.description: Fake MFA prompt shown after password capture — credential then MFA two-stage AiTM harvest
sourcePatterns.note: After capturing the password, showing a hidden MFA/verification code prompt is the two-stage AiTM credential+MFA harvest. The password is relayed to the real site, which triggers a real MFA challenge, then the phish captures the MFA code.

sourcePatterns.description: WebSocket streaming both credentials AND session tokens — real-time AiTM full-take exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: WebSocket connections streaming BOTH session tokens/cookies AND credentials simultaneously is the full-take AiTM exfiltration pattern. This enables real-time session hijacking where the attacker can use the victim's active session immediately.

sourcePatterns.description: postMessage listener capturing data from embedded auth iframe — cross-origin credential relay
sourcePatterns.note: postMessage listener extracting password/credential/SSN/card data. Narrowed from generic auth/token terms to definitive credential indicators.

sourcePatterns.description: Multi-step authentication state machine — manages email→password→MFA flow progression
sourcePatterns.note: A JavaScript state machine managing email→password→MFA step progression by showing/hiding page sections is the AiTM multi-step authentication flow controller. Each step captures different credentials and the kit advances through the auth flow.

sourcePatterns.description: Password input field without a 'Forgot password' or 'Reset password' link — missing expected UX element
sourcePatterns.note: A legitimate login page always has a 'Forgot password?' link. Phishing pages frequently omit this because they don't have a password reset backend. The absence of forgot/reset/recover near a password field is a simple but telling indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Form action pointing to a PHP file — common phishing credential collection endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: A login form posting to a PHP file is a classic phishing pattern. Modern legitimate services use API endpoints, not PHP scripts. PHP-based credential collectors remain the most common backend for low-sophistication phishing kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Autocomplete disabled on login form — prevents browser from recognizing this as a real login page
sourcePatterns.note: Setting autocomplete=off on a login form prevents the browser from offering saved credentials. Legitimate sites WANT autocomplete to work for user convenience. Phishing pages disable it to prevent the browser from alerting the user that this isn't the real site.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form without any 'Create account' or 'Sign up' link — harvesting-only page with no registration path
sourcePatterns.note: A login page with no 'Create account', 'Sign up', or 'Register' link is a harvesting-only page. Legitimate login pages always offer a registration path. Phishing pages only need the login form.

sourcePatterns.description: Address update form for supposed delivery problem — harvests full physical address and personal info
sourcePatterns.note: Address update forms claiming delivery failure due to incorrect/incomplete address harvest full physical addresses. The data enables identity theft, physical attacks, and targeted social engineering with the victim's real address.

sourcePatterns.description: Very small payment amount ($0.30-$4.99) with full card details required — micro-fee card harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Requesting full credit card details (card number, CVV, expiry) for a micro-payment of $0.30-$4.99 is the delivery fee scam signature. The small amount seems harmless but the goal is to capture the full card details for larger fraud.

sourcePatterns.description: Delivery preference or notification settings update page — harvests contact info and login credentials
sourcePatterns.note: Delivery preference/notification settings pages that ask for email, phone, or account credentials harvest contact information and login details under the guise of updating delivery preferences.

sourcePatterns.description: Package weight/dimensions form asking for payment — surcharge scam for oversized/overweight package
sourcePatterns.note: Surcharge pages claiming a package exceeds weight or size limits and requires additional payment are a delivery phishing variant. Real surcharges are handled by the sender, not collected via web pages from the recipient.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake tracking number lookup form that then asks for personal info — track-to-harvest pipeline
sourcePatterns.note: A fake tracking number input that leads to personal info or credential fields is the track-to-harvest pipeline. The victim enters a tracking number (any number works), then is asked for personal details or payment to 'release' the package.

sourcePatterns.description: Delivery OTP verification page — collects one-time code sent to phone for account verification
sourcePatterns.note: Delivery OTP verification pages claim a code was sent to the victim's phone to verify delivery. This is an MFA interception technique — the victim enters the real OTP from their carrier account, which the attacker uses for account takeover.

sourcePatterns.description: Small toll amount ($1.50-$12.50) with full payment card form — classic toll fee card harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: A small dollar amount ($1.50-$12.50) labeled as a toll balance with a full credit card form is the signature toll phishing card harvesting pattern. The amount is deliberately small to seem plausible but the real goal is capturing the full card details.

sourcePatterns.description: OTP/verification code requested after toll payment card entry — MFA interception for card fraud
sourcePatterns.note: Requesting an OTP/verification code AFTER the victim enters card details is the bank fraud escalation. The scammer initiates a real transaction with the stolen card and needs the victim's bank OTP to authorize it.

sourcePatterns.description: Apple Pay or Google Pay wallet linking prompt on toll payment page — mobile wallet fraud
sourcePatterns.note: Prompting victims to link a mobile wallet (Apple Pay, Google Pay) on a toll payment page is an emerging fraud vector. Scammers use the stolen card details to add the victim's card to their own mobile wallet for tap-to-pay fraud at physical stores.

sourcePatterns.description: License plate number input on toll payment page — vehicle identification harvesting
sourcePatterns.note: Collecting license plate numbers alongside toll payment details harvests vehicle identification data. Combined with name and address from the card, this gives attackers enough for vehicle registration fraud or targeted physical crimes.

sourcePatterns.description: Toll payment page with no account login option — direct card capture without authentication
sourcePatterns.note: A toll payment page that goes directly to card entry WITHOUT any account login is suspicious. Legitimate toll systems (EZPass, SunPass) require you to log into your account first. Going straight to card payment without auth is a phishing indicator.

sourcePatterns.description: Subscription cancellation form requesting login — credential harvest via cancel flow
sourcePatterns.note: Cancel subscription flows that require sign-in or identity verification harvest credentials under the guise of cancellation. The victim wants to cancel an unwanted charge, so they eagerly enter their credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Job offer or application form collecting excessive personal information — employment scam PII harvest
sourcePatterns.note: Job applications requesting SSN, date of birth, bank account, or passport information are employment scams harvesting PII for identity theft. Legitimate employers don't collect SSN or banking details in initial applications.

sourcePatterns.description: OAuth consent requesting broad permissions — malicious app authorization
sourcePatterns.note: Fake OAuth consent pages requesting broad permissions trick users into authorizing malicious apps with persistent account access.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft Graph API consent phishing — M365 data access requests
sourcePatterns.note: Microsoft Graph API permission scope names (Mail.Read, Mail.Send, etc.) in a consent page are definitive consent phishing indicators.

sourcePatterns.description: Common phishing kit typos in HTML — misspelled submit buttons and form labels
sourcePatterns.note: Common typos in phishing kits: 'sumbit' instead of 'submit', 'Sing In' instead of 'Sign In', 'Singin' instead of 'Sign in'. Legitimate production sites never have typos in their submit buttons. These typos persist because phishers clone pages hastily and don't QA.

sourcePatterns.description: Password show/hide toggle with type change from password to text — credential field inspection feature
sourcePatterns.note: Password show/hide toggle JavaScript that switches input type between 'password' and 'text' is a common feature cloned from legitimate login pages. While not malicious alone, its presence on a page with other phishing indicators confirms a cloned login page.

sourcePatterns.description: Form action to bare PHP filename without path — local phishing kit credential collector
sourcePatterns.note: A form posting to a bare PHP filename (post.php, login.php, submit.php, process.php) without any path indicates a locally-hosted phishing kit. The PHP file names are generic credential collectors. Legitimate services use API endpoints, not bare PHP filenames.

sourcePatterns.description: Remember me checkbox on phishing page — cloned UX element that serves no backend function
sourcePatterns.note: A 'Remember me' checkbox on a page that posts to a PHP file or sends to Telegram/Discord is a cloned UX element. The checkbox has no backend function on a phishing page — it's visual theater to make the form look legitimate.

sourcePatterns.description: Shadow form technique — JavaScript syncs visible input values into hidden form fields via querySelectorAll
sourcePatterns.note: The shadow form technique places visible credential inputs OUTSIDE the <form> element and hidden inputs INSIDE it. JavaScript copies the visible values into the hidden fields via querySelectorAll('input[type=hidden]').forEach(...). The form submits the hidden copies, not what the user typed into directly. This decouples the visual UI from the data submission.

sourcePatterns.description: Both onkeyup and onblur handlers on credential input — real-time keystroke tracking on login fields
sourcePatterns.note: Having BOTH onkeyup/oninput AND onblur event handlers on a credential field (text, password, or email input) means the value is captured on every keystroke AND when the field loses focus. This is real-time tracking — legitimate login forms use onsubmit, not per-keystroke capture. [Observation-level: onkeyup/onblur — standard form validation on every login page]

sourcePatterns.description: Credential inputs placed outside the form element — visual inputs decoupled from form submission
sourcePatterns.note: Credential input fields (text, password, email) with event handlers that appear BEFORE the <form> element, with hidden inputs inside the <form>, indicates the shadow form technique. The visible inputs aren't part of the form that gets submitted — their values are copied to hidden fields by JavaScript. [Observation-level: Input outside form — React controlled forms, Material UI, Ant Design]

sourcePatterns.description: Form action to /site/submit or similar generic collection endpoint — phishing platform credential receiver
sourcePatterns.note: A form posting to generic collection endpoints like /site/submit, /collect/creds, /log/data indicates a phishing platform's credential receiver. Legitimate services use meaningful API paths (/api/v1/auth, /login, /oauth/token), not generic collection endpoints.

sourcePatterns.description: Hidden field tracking whether password was entered (boolean) — phishing engagement metric, not credential capture
sourcePatterns.note: A hidden field named 'pass_entered' that stores 1 or 0 (boolean) instead of the actual password value is a phishing simulation or engagement tracking metric. It tracks WHETHER a password was entered, not what it was. This is distinctive to phishing platforms that measure user compliance.

sourcePatterns.description: Variables named for credential logging
sourcePatterns.note: Phishkit JS variable naming for credential accumulation

sourcePatterns.description: EmailJS API used to exfiltrate credentials via legitimate email service
sourcePatterns.note: EmailJS is a legitimate service increasingly abused by phishkits to exfiltrate stolen credentials via client-side JS. No legitimate login page sends passwords through EmailJS.

sourcePatterns.description: EmailJS send function called with form reference containing credential fields
sourcePatterns.note: Pattern matches emailjs.send(serviceId, templateId, formRef.current) — the exact API signature used in React-based phishkits for credential exfiltration via EmailJS.

sourcePatterns.description: EmailJS initialization with public key — used by phishkits for credential exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: EmailJS init with a public API key on a page with credentials suggests phishkit exfiltration. Moderate weight because EmailJS alone could be on legitimate contact forms.

sourcePatterns.description: Form submission to Formbold service with credential fields
sourcePatterns.note: Formbold is a form backend service abused by phishkits. A form posting to Formbold with a password field is near-definitive phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Form submission to Formspark service with credential fields
sourcePatterns.note: submit-form.com is Formspark's endpoint. A form posting passwords to this third-party service is near-definitive phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Image beacon exfiltration — creates new Image with credential data in URL
sourcePatterns.note: Image beacon is a classic exfiltration technique — creating an Image object with credential data encoded in the URL query string. No legitimate site does this.

sourcePatterns.description: FormData constructed and POSTed to a PHP endpoint — classic phishkit credential exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Constructing FormData and POSTing to a .php file is a classic phishkit pattern. Modern legitimate sites rarely use PHP backends for login forms.

sourcePatterns.description: XMLHttpRequest sending credential data via POST to external endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: XHR collecting password field values and sending them via POST is a direct credential exfiltration pattern found in numerous phishkits.

sourcePatterns.description: WebSocket used to exfiltrate credential data in real-time
sourcePatterns.note: WebSocket-based exfiltration sends stolen credentials in real-time to the attacker. Used by advanced phishkits for immediate session hijacking.

sourcePatterns.description: Credential form posting to submit-form.com (Formspark) endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: submit-form.com is Formspark's submission endpoint. When used as a form action, credentials entered are sent to the attacker's Formspark account.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirects to legitimate brand site after form submission — post-harvest redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Classic phishkit behavior: after stealing credentials, redirect the victim to the real brand site so they think the login failed or succeeded. Distinct from legitimate OAuth redirects.

sourcePatterns.description: Multi-step phishing form that collects password twice — email first, then password
sourcePatterns.note: Multi-stage phishing: collects email in step 1, password in step 2, sometimes MFA in step 3. The step counter variable is distinctive.

sourcePatterns.description: MFA/2FA/OTP code input field combined with credential harvesting signals
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing pages collecting MFA/2FA codes with 6-digit inputs and verify buttons. Distinct from legitimate MFA pages by context — this fires when other signals are also present.

sourcePatterns.description: Keypress event listener accumulating input characters for credential theft
sourcePatterns.note: Keylogger pattern: attaches keypress/keydown listener and accumulates each keystroke into a string or array. Used by advanced phishkits to capture credentials character by character.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form with empty or self-referential action combined with JS interception
sourcePatterns.note: Phishkits set the form action to #, javascript:, or empty so the form doesn't actually submit — instead, JS intercepts and exfiltrates the credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Form with password field posting to a different domain origin
sourcePatterns.note: A login form posting credentials to a different domain than expected. Excludes common legitimate auth providers. High weight because cross-origin password submission is a strong phishing signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple hidden input fields collecting browser/system metadata alongside credentials
sourcePatterns.note: Phishkits collect victim metadata (IP, browser, OS, location) in hidden form fields alongside credentials. Multiple such fields together are a strong signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Form posting to PHP script with brand name in the filename
sourcePatterns.note: Form posting to a PHP file containing a brand name (e.g., microsoft-login.php, paypal_verify.php) is a near-definitive phishkit signature.

sourcePatterns.description: Client-side password minimum length check typical of phishkit validation
sourcePatterns.note: Phishkits validate password length client-side and show error messages. While legitimate sites also validate, the specific pattern of checking and showing inline errors is common in kits.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form using GET method to send passwords — exposes credentials in URL
sourcePatterns.note: A login form using GET instead of POST sends the password in the URL query string. No legitimate site does this — it's a phishkit mistake that exposes the harvesting.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form with autocomplete disabled — prevents browser from warning about saved credentials
sourcePatterns.note: Disabling autocomplete on a login form prevents browsers from offering saved passwords, hiding the fact that the user has no saved credentials for this fake domain. Very low weight.

sourcePatterns.description: localStorage used to count credential submission attempts across page reloads
sourcePatterns.note: Phishkits use localStorage to persist attempt counters across page reloads. After N attempts, they redirect to the real site. This pattern is near-exclusive to phishkits.

sourcePatterns.description: SMTP.js library loaded on page impersonating Meta/Facebook business services
sourcePatterns.note: SMTP.js is used by phishkits for email-based credential exfiltration. Legitimate Meta services use internal APIs, never third-party email libraries.

sourcePatterns.description: Detects random subdomains on financial-sounding domains used for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Kept as regex — domain structure pattern matching inherently needs regex.

sourcePatterns.description: Vite build pattern with hashed asset names and minimal HTML commonly used for phishing SPAs on legitimate platforms
sourcePatterns.note: Vite bundler output pattern with hashed filenames - low weight as legitimate sites use this too, but useful as corroborating signal

sourcePatterns.description: Page content loaded from IPFS gateway — common decentralized phishing hosting
sourcePatterns.note: IPFS hosting is used by phishing kits because content is decentralized and takedown-resistant. While IPFS has legitimate uses, IPFS-hosted pages combined with other signals strongly indicate phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Login page content hosted on Backblaze B2 cloud storage — abused hosting platform
sourcePatterns.note: Backblaze B2 cloud storage has been observed hosting phishing pages, particularly Microsoft credential harvesting. The f00X.backblazeb2.com pattern is the B2 file serving format.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form resources loaded from Firebase storage — commonly abused cloud hosting
sourcePatterns.note: Firebase storage is heavily abused to host phishing page assets. Low weight because Firebase has many legitimate uses, but presence on a login page stacks with other hosting signals.

sourcePatterns.description: Login form resources loaded from Vercel app hosting — commonly abused free hosting platform
sourcePatterns.note: Vercel.app subdomains are heavily abused for hosting phishing pages and fake CAPTCHA gates. Low weight because Vercel has massive legitimate usage, but stacks with other signals on non-development pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Page or resources hosted on Netlify — commonly abused free hosting platform
sourcePatterns.note: Netlify.app subdomains are abused for phishing fake CAPTCHA pages. Low weight as supporting signal — Netlify has huge legitimate developer usage.

sourcePatterns.description: Page hosted on Render.com — free hosting abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Render.com free tier is abused for hosting phishing pages. Low weight as supporting signal due to legitimate developer usage.

sourcePatterns.description: Content loaded from Azure Blob Storage — abused cloud storage for phishing page hosting
sourcePatterns.note: Azure Blob Storage is heavily abused by Tycoon 2FA and other phishing kits. Phishing pages hosted on blob.core.windows.net inherit Microsoft's trusted reputation. Moderate weight because it also has legitimate uses.

sourcePatterns.description: Content served from Cloudflare Workers — serverless hosting abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Cloudflare Workers (.workers.dev) provide serverless hosting that is abused for phishing proxies and page hosting. Low weight as supporting signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Content served from Cloudflare Pages — static hosting abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Cloudflare Pages (.pages.dev) free hosting is abused for phishing sites. Low weight because of massive legitimate usage.

sourcePatterns.description: Content hosted on Surge.sh — free static hosting abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Surge.sh is a free static hosting service with no abuse controls, making it popular for phishing deployment. Higher weight than Vercel/Netlify because Surge has less legitimate mainstream usage.

sourcePatterns.description: Google AMP URL used as phishing redirect proxy — abuses Google's trusted domain
sourcePatterns.note: Google AMP URLs proxy content through Google's domain, inheriting trust and bypassing domain-based filters. Phishing campaigns use AMP as a redirect layer.

sourcePatterns.description: Content hosted on Cloudflare R2 storage — object storage abused for phishing assets
sourcePatterns.note: Cloudflare R2 storage (.r2.dev) is an S3-compatible object store abused for hosting phishing page assets. Moderate weight as it has less legitimate public-facing usage than R2 custom domains.

sourcePatterns.description: Page hosted on Weebly website builder — commonly abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Weebly free sites are commonly used to host phishing pages. The drag-and-drop builder makes it easy to create convincing brand impersonation pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Content loaded from Google Cloud Storage — abused cloud object storage
sourcePatterns.note: Google Cloud Storage (storage.googleapis.com) is abused to host phishing page HTML and assets. The trusted Google domain helps phishing pages bypass reputation-based filters.

sourcePatterns.description: Content loaded from AWS S3 bucket — abused cloud storage for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: AWS S3 buckets are used by Tycoon 2FA and other kits to host phishing pages, especially using regional endpoints like s3.ap-northeast-3.amazonaws.com. Trusted AWS infrastructure bypasses many filters.

sourcePatterns.description: Content loaded from Linode Object Storage — abused cloud hosting
sourcePatterns.note: Linode Object Storage is heavily abused for hosting phishing pages, particularly Microsoft credential harvesting. Higher weight than major cloud providers because Linode objects have less legitimate public-facing web content.

sourcePatterns.description: Content served through ngrok tunnel — local phishing server exposed via tunnel
sourcePatterns.note: Ngrok tunnels expose local servers to the internet. Phishing kits use ngrok to run credential harvesting servers locally while making them accessible via a seemingly legitimate ngrok.io URL.

sourcePatterns.description: Page hosted on Glitch.me — free hosting platform abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Glitch.me free hosting is abused for phishing pages. The platform allows rapid deployment of server-side code including PHP-like credential handlers.

sourcePatterns.description: Content loaded from Arweave permanent storage — decentralized hosting immune to takedown
sourcePatterns.note: Arweave provides permanent, immutable storage. Phishing pages hosted on Arweave cannot be taken down by anyone — they exist forever on the permaweb. The 43-character transaction ID identifies each stored page.

sourcePatterns.description: Content loaded from Filecoin/IPFS gateways — decentralized storage for phishing persistence
sourcePatterns.note: Web3 storage gateways (w3s.link, nftstorage.link) serve content from IPFS/Filecoin networks. These are increasingly used for takedown-resistant phishing hosting.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord CDN used to host or serve phishing assets — attachment URL abuse
sourcePatterns.note: Discord CDN attachment URLs (cdn.discordapp.com/attachments/) are abused to host phishing page assets, malware payloads, and images. The CDN inherits Discord's trusted reputation.

sourcePatterns.description: File hosting shared link leading to credential page — Google Drive, OneDrive, Dropbox abuse
sourcePatterns.note: File hosting service links on pages with credential text. 26% of phishing links use file hosting services.

sourcePatterns.description: Single-line HTML loading index-[hash].js — BlackForce phishkit entry point pattern
sourcePatterns.note: BlackForce phishkit uses a single script tag loading index-[hash].js as its entire entry point. The cache-busting hash forces fresh downloads of the malicious bundle.

sourcePatterns.description: Cloudflare Pages hosting a page with credential form
sourcePatterns.note: Cloudflare Pages/Workers domains hosting login forms is a common phishing infrastructure pattern. Moderate weight because some legitimate dev sites use these.

sourcePatterns.description: Glitch.me hosting a page with credential fields
sourcePatterns.note: Glitch.me free hosting is frequently abused for phishing. A password field on a Glitch-hosted page is suspicious.

sourcePatterns.description: Replit.dev hosting a page with credential fields
sourcePatterns.note: Replit free hosting frequently abused for credential phishing. Password field on Replit is suspicious.

sourcePatterns.description: Weebly hosting a page with credential harvesting indicators
sourcePatterns.note: Weebly free sites are a common phishing infrastructure. Login forms on Weebly are suspicious since most Weebly sites are content pages, not auth portals.

sourcePatterns.description: Cloudflare R2 storage URL used to host page assets
sourcePatterns.note: Cloudflare R2 storage with random hex bucket names is used by phishkits for hosting. Low weight as R2 has legitimate uses.

sourcePatterns.description: Cloudflare Worker URL pattern used as phishing reverse proxy
sourcePatterns.note: Cloudflare Workers with subdomain patterns used as reverse proxies for credential phishing. Common in Tycoon 2FA infrastructure.

sourcePatterns.description: Amazon S3 bucket hosting phishing page content
sourcePatterns.note: Amazon S3 buckets hosting phishing pages are a well-documented infrastructure pattern, especially for Tycoon 2FA campaigns.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram Bot API credential exfiltration — any send method with bot token
sourcePatterns.note: Comprehensive Telegram exfil detection: requires the API domain AND a valid bot token AND any one of the send methods. Replaces 4 separate legacy rules with one multi-match rule. The fast_pattern on 'api.telegram.org' means this rule is only evaluated on pages that reference Telegram's API.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook credential exfiltration — webhook URL with data payload
sourcePatterns.note: Discord webhook exfil: requires the webhook URL pattern AND a valid webhook ID/token AND a fetch mechanism. The webhook ID (17-20 digits) and token (60+ chars) format is definitive.

sourcePatterns.description: ClickFix paste-and-run attack — fake CAPTCHA with clipboard PowerShell injection
sourcePatterns.note: ClickFix attack: clipboard manipulation AND a system command (powershell or mshta) AND verification language (verify, captcha, or human). 47% of attacks per Microsoft use this technique.

sourcePatterns.description: Tycoon 2FA multi-stage flow — email validation then password then MFA capture
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA multi-stage flow: requires password + email + display toggling + (otp OR authenticator OR verification code) + stage naming. This catches the full email→password→MFA progression.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft login impersonation on non-Microsoft domain — with FP exclusion for real Microsoft
sourcePatterns.note: Fires on pages mentioning Microsoft + password + form, but NOT on real Microsoft domains. The four negated matches ensure this never fires on login.microsoftonline.com, microsoft.com, office.com, or live.com. Previously impossible with a single regex.

sourcePatterns.description: Invoice/remittance document lure with credential harvest — with FP exclusion for real accounting software
sourcePatterns.note: Invoice document lure with password field, but NOT on real accounting platforms (QuickBooks, FreshBooks, Xero, Wave). This is the FP-safe version of invoice phishing detection — a real QuickBooks invoice page mentions 'invoice' and might have a login form, but won't be flagged.

sourcePatterns.description: Dean Edwards JavaScript Packer — complete detection with eval, function signature, and dictionary
sourcePatterns.note: Dean Edwards Packer: both the eval(function(p,a,c,k,e, signature AND the .split('|') dictionary unpacker must be present. Using content matches instead of regex — much faster and equally precise since these are exact string literals.

sourcePatterns.description: SVG file with embedded phishing payload — foreignObject HTML form or onload redirect
sourcePatterns.note: SVG phishing: requires <svg> AND either (foreignObject with form/password) OR (onload with redirect). Two independent detection paths in one rule.

sourcePatterns.description: Unicode character substitution in brand names and login text
Detects phishing pages that use an empty container div with a large encrypted/base64 payload in JS for runtime rendering — common in Cloudflare Workers phishkits
Form auto-submitted via string manipulation to obfuscate the .submit() call — phishkit anti-detection technique
Detects pages with multiple randomized CSS class names using display:none to implement character-interleaving obfuscation, common in Cloudflare impersonation phishkits
Detects phishkits that base64-encode entire page HTML, apply character-position obfuscation, then document.write the decoded result
Obfuscated page using __SCRIPT_PLACEHOLDER__ tokens replaced at runtime before document.write, characteristic of credential phishing kits
Detects base64 decode + character splice deobfuscation followed by document.write, common in phishing loaders
Detects pages where entire content is base64-decoded and written to DOM via document.write(unescape(atob())) — common phishkit obfuscation
Base64 decode followed by XOR deobfuscation — multi-layer payload hiding used by Whisper 2FA and similar kits
sourcePatterns.note: The atob() + charCodeAt() + XOR chain is the signature multi-layer deobfuscation technique used by Whisper 2FA and BlackForce phishing kits. Legitimate sites don't chain base64 decode with XOR character manipulation.

sourcePatterns.description: Bulk String.fromCharCode calls used to reconstruct obfuscated page content or URLs
sourcePatterns.note: Bulk String.fromCharCode with 5+ comma-separated character codes reconstructs obfuscated strings at runtime. Very common in phishing kits to hide URLs, brand names, and credential harvesting code from static analysis.

sourcePatterns.description: Heavy hex escape sequences in JavaScript — used to hide phishing keywords from static scanners
sourcePatterns.note: Heavy hex escape obfuscation — 10+ consecutive hex escapes. Raised from 5 — minified JS commonly has short hex runs.

sourcePatterns.description: Document.write with unescape chain to inject decoded HTML — phishing page loader technique
sourcePatterns.note: document.write(unescape()) is a legacy but still common phishing page injection technique. The entire page HTML is URL-encoded and decoded at runtime to evade static analysis. Modern legitimate sites never use this pattern.

sourcePatterns.description: Blob URL created and used as iframe source — phishing content injection technique
sourcePatterns.note: Creating a Blob URL and assigning it to an iframe's src is a known phishing evasion technique that hides phishing content from static URL analysis. The content exists only in the browser's memory. [Observation-level: Blob URL iframe — PDF viewers, sandboxed content, Webpack]

sourcePatterns.description: Form action attribute set dynamically via decoded/deobfuscated value — hides exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Dynamically setting a form's action attribute using a decoded value hides the exfiltration endpoint from static HTML analysis. The form action appears blank in source but is set at runtime.

sourcePatterns.description: Full page HTML content injected via document.write from a variable — phishing page loader
sourcePatterns.note: Storing an entire HTML document in a JavaScript variable and writing it to the page via document.write is a phishing page injection technique. The real page content is hidden in a script variable and rendered at runtime.

sourcePatterns.description: AES decryption used to deobfuscate page content at runtime — advanced phishing kit technique
sourcePatterns.note: Using CryptoJS AES decryption to decode page content at runtime is an advanced phishing kit technique (seen in CarPhish kit). The encrypted payload evades all static analysis. The decrypt-then-render chain is definitive.

sourcePatterns.description: Number.toString with custom radix used for string obfuscation — hides phishing content
sourcePatterns.note: Radix obfuscation — 3+ toString(16/36) calls in close proximity. Raised from 2 — analytics code commonly uses hex conversion.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple .replace() calls chained to deobfuscate strings — phishing payload decoder
sourcePatterns.note: Four or more chained .replace() calls indicate string transformation for deobfuscation. Phishing kits use this to reconstruct URLs and HTML content from scrambled representations.

sourcePatterns.description: Array reversed and joined to decode obfuscated strings — simple but common phishkit technique
sourcePatterns.note: Splitting a string into characters, reversing, and joining is a basic phishkit obfuscation technique. Strings like 'moc.elgoog' become 'google.com' after this operation. Simple but still widely used.

sourcePatterns.description: LZ-string library used to decompress obfuscated JavaScript payload — Tycoon 2FA signature technique
sourcePatterns.note: LZ-string compression is the payload delivery mechanism used by Tycoon 2FA phishing kit. The initial page includes a base64 payload compressed with LZ-string, which is decompressed at runtime to reveal the phishing page. Legitimate sites rarely use LZ-string for page rendering.

sourcePatterns.description: Invisible Unicode characters (Hangul fillers) used for JavaScript obfuscation — Tycoon 2FA evasion technique
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA uses Hangul Filler (U+3164) and Halfwidth Hangul Filler (U+FFA0) characters to encode binary data invisibly. Eight or more consecutive invisible Unicode characters in JavaScript is a definitive obfuscation signal. No legitimate code uses these characters.

sourcePatterns.description: Iframe dynamically created and injected to load phishing content from external source — GhostFrame technique
sourcePatterns.note: Dynamically creating an iframe and setting its src to an external URL (or decoded URL) is the GhostFrame phishing technique. The outer HTML appears harmless while the iframe loads the actual phishing content. [Observation-level: Dynamic iframe — payment widgets, embeds, analytics]

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript Proxy objects used for property access obfuscation — advanced evasion technique
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript Proxy objects combined with decoding functions create a dynamic property access layer that hides the actual code being executed. Tycoon 2FA uses this with invisible Unicode characters for multi-layer obfuscation.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake or empty sourceMappingURL used to confuse debuggers
sourcePatterns.note: Embedding a fake or malformed sourceMappingURL confuses browser DevTools by pointing to non-existent or garbage source maps. This is a minor evasion technique that makes debugging more difficult.

sourcePatterns.description: Recursive setTimeout chain used to delay and stage payload execution
sourcePatterns.note: Three or more nested setTimeout calls create a delayed execution chain that stages payload delivery over time. This evades scanners that only analyze the initial page state and miss delayed content injection. [Observation-level: Recursive setTimeout — polling patterns, heartbeats, animations]

sourcePatterns.description: Brand names encoded as HTML entities to evade text-based detection — obfuscation technique
sourcePatterns.note: Encoding 'Microsoft' as HTML entity sequences (Microsoft) evades text-based brand detection. The browser renders it as 'Microsoft' but string matching on the source fails.

sourcePatterns.description: Content Security Policy meta tag with permissive values to enable inline scripts
sourcePatterns.note: A CSP meta tag explicitly allowing both unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval is common on phishing pages that need to execute inline obfuscated JavaScript. Legitimate sites typically restrict these capabilities.

sourcePatterns.description: URL reconstructed from hex-encoded character pairs — hides exfiltration endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: Reconstructing URLs by parsing hex pairs and converting to characters via fromCharCode hides the exfiltration destination from static analysis. The URL only exists at runtime.

sourcePatterns.description: XOR cipher with string key used to decode phishing page content at runtime
sourcePatterns.note: XOR decryption using a repeating key string where each byte is XORed with the corresponding key byte (using modulo) is a signature obfuscation technique in Whisper 2FA and similar kits.

sourcePatterns.description: SVG file with embedded JavaScript or foreignObject containing phishing form — SVG-based attack
sourcePatterns.note: SVG files can contain foreignObject elements with full HTML forms or embedded JavaScript. This attack vector bypasses many file type filters because SVG appears to be an image format.

sourcePatterns.description: SVG with embedded JavaScript redirect — phishing redirect disguised as image
sourcePatterns.note: SVG files with embedded script or animate elements that redirect the browser are used as phishing redirectors. The SVG appears to be an image attachment but executes JavaScript when opened.

sourcePatterns.description: Image onerror handler used to execute JavaScript — stealthy script execution technique
sourcePatterns.note: Using an img tag with an intentionally broken src and a long onerror handler is a technique to execute JavaScript without a <script> tag. The 'image' never loads, triggering the onerror which contains the phishing payload.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS content property used to inject visible text — evades text-based scanning
sourcePatterns.note: Using CSS content property to inject visible text like 'Sign In' or 'Verify Your Account' evades text-based phishing detection. The phishing text doesn't exist in the HTML — it's rendered purely via CSS.

sourcePatterns.description: Page contains extremely large base64-encoded inline images — anti-analysis code inflation
sourcePatterns.note: Pages with very large base64-encoded inline images (10,000+ chars) inflate the page source to make analysis difficult. The images may be brand logos or backgrounds embedded to avoid external asset loading that could be tracked. [Observation-level: base64 images — every page with inline SVGs, data URIs]

sourcePatterns.description: HTMX library used to load phishing form dynamically via AJAX — client-side cloaking
sourcePatterns.note: Using HTMX attributes (hx-get, hx-post, hx-trigger) to dynamically load phishing form content is a client-side cloaking technique. The initial page appears clean; the phishing form loads via AJAX after a user action.

sourcePatterns.description: Zero-width characters inserted in brand text to evade string matching
sourcePatterns.note: Multiple zero-width characters (ZWSP, ZWNJ, ZWJ, BOM) inserted between visible characters break string matching while rendering normally in the browser. Text like 'M‌i‌c‌r‌o‌s‌o‌f‌t' with ZWNJs is invisible to pattern matchers but displays as 'Microsoft'.

sourcePatterns.description: Right-to-left text direction override used to disguise file extensions or URLs
sourcePatterns.note: The Right-to-Left Override character (U+202E) reverses the visual display of text, used to disguise file extensions (exe.pdf appears as fdp.exe) or URLs. Multiple RTL overrides in page content is a definitive obfuscation signal.

sourcePatterns.description: Internationalized domain names using homoglyph characters in page source
sourcePatterns.note: Punycode-encoded internationalized domain names (xn--...) in page source links indicate homoglyph domain abuse. These domains use look-alike Unicode characters to impersonate legitimate brands.

sourcePatterns.description: Aria-hidden elements containing phishing content invisible to accessibility tools
sourcePatterns.note: Using aria-hidden=true on elements containing login/password text hides phishing content from screen readers and accessibility-based scanners while keeping it visually visible to sighted users.

sourcePatterns.description: Canvas pixel data extraction for steganographic payload decoding — hidden data in images
sourcePatterns.note: Extracting pixel data from a canvas element and converting RGB values to characters is steganographic payload decoding. Malicious JavaScript or phishing HTML is hidden in image pixel values and decoded at runtime.

sourcePatterns.description: Body content hidden via CSS with JavaScript redirect — Mamba 2FA HTML attachment technique
sourcePatterns.note: Mamba 2FA HTML attachments use body{display:none} to hide benign filler content, with a script that decodes and redirects to the phishing page. The hidden body + decoded redirect combination is a definitive Mamba 2FA attachment signature.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram bot token split across multiple variables and reassembled — deep obfuscation
sourcePatterns.note: Splitting the Telegram bot token across multiple variables (part1='12345', part2='6789:', part3='AAAA...') and concatenating them is advanced obfuscation. The token never appears as a complete string in the source code.

sourcePatterns.description: Discord webhook URL constructed from hex-encoded characters — deep obfuscation
sourcePatterns.note: Reconstructing 'discord' from hex escape sequences (\x64\x69\x73\x63\x6f\x72\x64) or fromCharCode is an obfuscation technique to avoid string matching on the Discord webhook URL.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram API URL stored as reversed string and flipped at runtime — string reversal obfuscation
sourcePatterns.note: Storing Telegram API keywords reversed ('gro.margolet' = 'telegram.org', 'egasseMdnes' = 'sendMessage') and reversing them at runtime is a string reversal obfuscation technique used by phishing kits.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator string array rotation — IIFE with push/shift loop on string array
sourcePatterns.note: The string array rotation is the #1 fingerprint of javascript-obfuscator output. It uses _0xNNNN['push'](_0xNNNN['shift']()) in a while(true) loop to rotate the string array by a computed offset. This exact pattern appears in virtually all obfuscator.io output.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator string array accessor function — maps hex index to array lookup
sourcePatterns.note: The string array accessor function is a core component of javascript-obfuscator. It takes a hex index, subtracts an offset, and returns the corresponding string from the shuffled array. The _0xNNNN = _0xNNNN - 0xNN pattern is definitive.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator parseInt chain — multiple parseInt calls with hex arguments for rotation calculation
sourcePatterns.note: The string array rotation calculation uses a chain of parseInt(_0xAccessor(0xHEX))/0xHEX operations with arithmetic operators. Two or more of these in sequence is a definitive obfuscator.io rotation verifier.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator boolean encoding — !![] used as replacement for true literal
sourcePatterns.note: while(!![]) { ... push/shift } is the obfuscator.io string array rotation loop. !![] evaluates to true in JavaScript. While !![] alone is too common (used in minified code), !![] combined with push/shift or _0x variables is definitive.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator control flow flattening — pipe-delimited switch order string
sourcePatterns.note: Control flow flattening transforms sequential code into a switch statement with pipe-delimited order strings like '2|0|4|1|3'. The _0xAccessor(0xHEX)['split']('|') pattern extracts the execution order from the obfuscated string array.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator self-defending — Function.toString() integrity check preventing code modification
sourcePatterns.note: Function("return this")() + toString — used by some legitimate polyfills. Lowered weight — weak obfuscation indicator, not definitive.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator dead code injection — random hex strings interspersed as string array entries
sourcePatterns.note: Dead code injection fills the string array with random entries like '133039ViRMWR', '1243717qSZCyh'. These follow a pattern of numbers followed by 5-7 mixed-case letters. Three or more in a row in an array is a strong obfuscator.io marker.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator bracket notation — property access via string array lookup instead of dot notation
sourcePatterns.note: Bracket notation property access via string array lookups like obj[_0xNNNN(0xHH)] replaces all dot notation. Two bracket-notation lookups using _0x accessor functions within 50 chars of each other indicates heavy obfuscator.io usage.

sourcePatterns.description: High density of hex number literals — characteristic of javascript-obfuscator numbersToExpressions
sourcePatterns.note: Five hex number literals (0x1a3, 0x13a, etc.) within 150 chars of each other indicates javascript-obfuscator's numbersToExpressions and hex literal encoding. Normal JavaScript code rarely has this density of hex numbers.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator wrapper IIFE — characteristic (function(_0xHEX, _0xHEX) entry structure
sourcePatterns.note: The wrapper IIFE that initializes the string array rotation has a very specific structure: (function(_0xHEX, _0xHEX){ var _0xHEX = _0xAccessor... This exact pattern of hex-named parameters with an immediate accessor variable is definitive obfuscator.io.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator RC4 string encoding — RC4 cipher used to encrypt string array values
sourcePatterns.note: The RC4 string encoding option encrypts each string in the array with RC4 cipher. The decryption function contains charCodeAt, fromCharCode, and modulo 256 (0x100) operations — these combined in the context of _0x variables indicate RC4 string decoding.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator string array calls wrapper — function returning the string array reference
sourcePatterns.note: The string array is wrapped in a function like function _0xNNNN(){ var _0xNNNN = ['string1', 'string2', ...]; ...}. This wrapper function is the second core structural element of obfuscator.io output (after the accessor function).

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple _0x hex-prefixed function parameters — javascript-obfuscator identifier renaming
sourcePatterns.note: Functions with three or more _0x hex-prefixed parameters indicate javascript-obfuscator's identifier renaming with hexadecimal generator. Normal code never uses multiple _0x prefixed identifiers as function parameters.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator unicode escape sequences — \uNNNN encoding for all string values
sourcePatterns.note: Five or more consecutive \uNNNN unicode escape sequences indicate obfuscator.io's unicodeEscapeSequence option. All string literals are converted to unicode escapes, hiding brand names, URLs, and keywords.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator numbersToExpressions — numeric constants replaced with arithmetic expressions
sourcePatterns.note: numbersToExpressions replaces numeric constants like 123 with equivalent expressions like 683517 ^ 683398 or 100 + 20 + 3. Chains of arithmetic producing simple numbers, especially using XOR (^) or unsigned right shift (>>>0x0), are obfuscator.io markers.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator splitStrings — long strings split into concatenated fragments in array
sourcePatterns.note: splitStrings breaks string literals into small fragments joined by concatenation ('he' + 'll' + 'o'). Five or more 1-5 char fragments concatenated indicates obfuscator.io's string splitting. This makes string matching nearly impossible.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator debugProtection — setInterval with debugger statement on 4000ms cycle
sourcePatterns.note: debugProtection uses setInterval with a 4000ms (0xfa0) or 5000ms (0x1388) cycle to continuously trigger debugger statements. The interval + debugger + hex timing constant is a definitive obfuscator.io debug protection signature. [Observation-level: Debug protection — obfuscator.io default, used by some SaaS apps]

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator base64 string array encoding — atob used in string accessor with caching
sourcePatterns.note: The base64 string encoding option wraps the string accessor with atob() decoding and a cache object (initialized as {}). The cache prevents repeated decoding of the same index. The atob + empty cache object + _0x accessor chain is definitive.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator self-defending counter — tracks if code has been reformatted
sourcePatterns.note: Self-defending mode tracks whether the code is being run for the first time and counts modification attempts. The firstCall/counter pattern with debug references creates an infinite loop or crash if the code has been beautified.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator console output disable — overwrites console methods with empty functions
sourcePatterns.note: javascript-obfuscator's disableConsoleOutput option replaces all console methods (log, warn, error, info, debug, trace, table, dir) with empty functions. This prevents any debug output from appearing during execution. [Observation-level: Console disable — obfuscator.io default output]

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator domainLock — restricts code execution to specific domains
sourcePatterns.note: domainLock restricts obfuscated code to specific domains. If the hostname doesn't match, the code enters an infinite loop or triggers debugger. The domain check + infinite loop/debugger fallback is a strong obfuscator.io indicator. [Observation-level: Domain lock — obfuscator.io feature, used by legitimate paid scripts]

sourcePatterns.description: High density of _0x prefixed identifiers — javascript-obfuscator hexadecimal identifier generator
sourcePatterns.note: Four or more _0x hex identifiers within 80 chars indicates pervasive javascript-obfuscator identifier renaming. The existing obfuscator-io-signature rule catches individual _0x variables; this catches the density pattern.

sourcePatterns.description: Large string array at script start — javascript-obfuscator string array with 20+ encoded entries
sourcePatterns.note: A large array of encoded strings (200+ chars) assigned to a _0x variable at the start of a script is the string array that stores all obfuscated string literals. This is the most recognizable structural element of obfuscator.io output.

sourcePatterns.description: javascript-obfuscator transformObjectKeys — object property names replaced with string array lookups
sourcePatterns.note: transformObjectKeys replaces object literal property names with string array lookups: { [_0xNNNN(0xHH)]: value, [_0xNNNN(0xHH)]: value }. Two or more computed property names using _0x accessors in the same object indicate this transform.

sourcePatterns.description: WebAssembly module instantiated on page — potential WASM-based credential logic hiding
sourcePatterns.note: WebAssembly instantiation on a login page moves credential processing logic into compiled WASM bytecode that regex patterns cannot inspect. While WASM has legitimate uses, its presence on a credential page combined with other signals is suspicious.

sourcePatterns.description: Large blocks of junk/dead code between functional elements — proximity rule evasion padding
sourcePatterns.note: Five or more consecutive junk variable declarations with short string values are padding inserted to push functional code elements apart, defeating proximity-bounded regex rules. The padding inflates distance between exfil setup and credential reading.

sourcePatterns.description: React dangerouslySetInnerHTML injecting login form content — framework-based credential cloaking
sourcePatterns.note: Using React's dangerouslySetInnerHTML to inject login form content renders the form through React's virtual DOM, hiding it from static source analysis. The HTML containing the credential form exists only as a JavaScript string.

sourcePatterns.description: eval() executing dynamically constructed string — runtime code generation hiding phishing logic
sourcePatterns.note: eval() with three or more concatenated variables constructs the evaluated code at runtime from fragments. The actual phishing logic never exists as a complete string in the source — it's assembled from pieces and evaluated.

sourcePatterns.description: new Function() with concatenated string — constructor-based dynamic code execution
sourcePatterns.note: Using the Function constructor with concatenated strings is equivalent to eval() but harder to detect. The Function('return ' + x + y + z)() pattern assembles and executes phishing code at runtime.

sourcePatterns.description: setTimeout with string argument — evaluates string as code, hiding phishing logic in string data
sourcePatterns.note: setTimeout with a string argument (not a function) evaluates the string as code after the delay. Phishing kits embed their logic as a long string in setTimeout to defer execution and hide code from static analysis. [Observation-level: setTimeout with string — old but legitimate code pattern]

sourcePatterns.description: Indirect eval via window['eval'] or this['eval'] or (0,eval) — evasion of direct eval detection
sourcePatterns.note: Indirect eval access via bracket notation (window['eval']), comma operator ((0,eval)), or globalThis avoids direct 'eval(' pattern matching. The indirect form functions identically but evades simple detection rules.

sourcePatterns.description: Prototype pollution used to override native methods for covert exfiltration
sourcePatterns.note: Overriding native XMLHttpRequest/fetch/sendBeacon prototypes lets phishing kits intercept all network requests silently. The overridden method exfiltrates data before passing through to the original, hiding exfil in normal traffic.

sourcePatterns.description: Page with no external scripts but very large inline script — self-contained obfuscated phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: A page with a very large (5000+ char) inline script and no external script sources is characteristic of self-contained phishing pages where all code is embedded and typically obfuscated. Legitimate pages usually load scripts from external CDNs.

sourcePatterns.description: Whitespace character encoding — tabs, spaces, and non-breaking spaces used as binary encoding for payloads
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon 2FA whitespace char encoding — charCodeAt + fromCharCode + Unicode whitespace escapes.

sourcePatterns.description: AES decryption of phishing page payload at runtime — Tycoon's encrypted payload delivery (May 2025+)
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon's May 2025+ versions encrypt the entire phishing page payload with AES. The encrypted blob is decrypted at runtime and injected into the DOM. The AES decrypt → DOM injection chain is the key signature.

sourcePatterns.description: JSFuck/JSFireTruck encoding — JavaScript written using only []()!+ characters
sourcePatterns.note: JSFuck encodes ANY JavaScript using only 6 characters: []()!+. The pattern [][(![]+[])[+[]]+([![]]+[][[]])... is the definitive opening of JSFuck-encoded code. Unit 42 reported a widespread JSFireTruck (JSFuck variant) campaign starting April 2025 infecting many websites.

sourcePatterns.description: High density of JSFuck character patterns — long sequences of only []()!+ characters
sourcePatterns.note: Ten or more JSFuck primitives (+[], !![], ![], +!+[], [+!+[]]) in sequence indicates JSFuck-encoded content. JSFuck is extremely verbose — even simple code becomes thousands of characters of these patterns.

sourcePatterns.description: JJEncode obfuscation — JavaScript encoded using $=~[] notation and Japanese characters
sourcePatterns.note: JJEncode starts with the distinctive $=~[];$={ initialization sequence. This encoding technique uses only 18 symbols ([]()!+,"$.:;_{}~=) to encode arbitrary JavaScript. It's been used in phishing since 2013 and still appears in web compromises.

sourcePatterns.description: AAEncode obfuscation — JavaScript encoded using Japanese emoticon characters (゜ω゜ノ)
sourcePatterns.note: AAEncode uses Japanese emoticon characters (゜ω゜ or ゜Д゜) to encode JavaScript. The ゜ω゜ or ゜Д゜ character sequences are the definitive AAEncode signature. While less common than JSFuck, AAEncode appears in web-based attacks.

sourcePatterns.description: Dean Edwards JavaScript Packer — eval(function(p,a,c,k,e,d) signature
sourcePatterns.note: Dean Edwards' JavaScript Packer output starts with eval(function(p,a,c,k,e,d) or eval(function(p,a,c,k,e,r). This is one of the oldest and most widely recognized JavaScript packers, still heavily used in phishing kits and web compromises.

sourcePatterns.description: Dean Edwards Packer base62 decode pattern — string replacement with base62 dictionary
sourcePatterns.note: The Dean Edwards Packer decodes its payload by splitting a pipe-delimited dictionary and replacing word boundaries (\b\w+\b) with dictionary entries. This split('|') + replace(\b\w+\b) chain is the definitive Packer unpacking mechanism.

sourcePatterns.description: Script loaded from data: URI — encoded JavaScript payload in data URI scheme
sourcePatterns.note: Loading a script from a data: URI embeds the entire JavaScript payload inline as a base64 or URL-encoded string. This bypasses external script loading detection and CSP source restrictions that allow data: URIs.

sourcePatterns.description: Blob URL created and used as script source — runtime-generated code URL
sourcePatterns.note: Creating a Blob URL from JavaScript code and using it as a script source generates code at runtime that can't be analyzed statically. The code only exists as a Blob in memory, never as a static file or inline script.

sourcePatterns.description: Dynamic import() loading external JavaScript module at runtime — deferred code loading
sourcePatterns.note: Using dynamic import() with a decoded or constructed URL loads JavaScript modules at runtime. The module URL is obfuscated (base64, concatenated, etc.) so the true source can't be identified by static analysis. [Observation-level: Dynamic import() — code splitting in Webpack, Vite, Next.js]

sourcePatterns.description: Tagged template literal used for code execution — Function or eval invoked via template tag
sourcePatterns.note: Using tagged template literals to invoke Function, eval, setTimeout, or setInterval (e.g., Function`return this`()) is an unusual execution pattern that bypasses keyword-based detection. The backtick syntax looks different from normal function calls.

sourcePatterns.description: CSS content property used to store encoded data extracted by JavaScript — CSS-based data hiding
sourcePatterns.note: Storing encoded data in CSS content properties and extracting it with getComputedStyle().content hides payloads in CSS where JavaScript analysis tools don't look. The data is decoded and executed after extraction.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript 'with' statement used for scope manipulation — obscures property access chains
sourcePatterns.note: The 'with' statement extends the scope chain, allowing property access without explicitly naming the object. with(document){ write(cookie) } is equivalent to document.write(document.cookie) but hides the explicit document references from pattern matching.

sourcePatterns.description: arguments.callee used for self-referencing function — anti-analysis recursive obfuscation
sourcePatterns.note: Using arguments.callee to reference the current function enables self-modifying and self-verifying code patterns. Combined with toString(), it creates integrity checks that break if the function is modified or deobfuscated.

sourcePatterns.description: String constructor used to build and execute code — [].constructor.constructor pattern
sourcePatterns.note: The [].constructor.constructor('code')() or ''.constructor.constructor('code')() pattern accesses the Function constructor through prototype chains without ever writing the word 'Function'. This is the core mechanism behind JSFuck-style code execution.

sourcePatterns.description: Number.toString with custom radix to build strings — radix encoding for character generation
sourcePatterns.note: Using Number.toString(radix) with high radix values (20-36) to generate alphabet characters is a string-building technique. For example, (35).toString(36) returns 'z'. Chaining these builds strings without any string literals in the source.

sourcePatterns.description: Metasploit JSObfu output pattern — nested anonymous function wrappers with xor encoding
sourcePatterns.note: Metasploit's JSObfu (JavaScript Obfuscator) wraps code in nested anonymous functions with a global reference variable, XOR decoding, and eval/Function execution. JSObfu is used in penetration testing but also adopted by real attackers.

sourcePatterns.description: Nested eval() calls — multi-layer eval unwrapping for deeply obfuscated payloads
sourcePatterns.note: Nested eval(eval(...)) or eval(atob(eval(...))) creates multiple layers of unwrapping. Each eval layer decodes the next, requiring multiple deobfuscation passes. Two or more eval() calls within 200 chars indicates multi-layer obfuscation.

sourcePatterns.description: charCodeAt in loop with XOR — runtime string decryption using character-level XOR cipher
sourcePatterns.note: A loop iterating over characters with charCodeAt, XOR (^), and fromCharCode is a character-level XOR decryption routine. This pattern decodes encoded strings at runtime, where the XOR key can be any value.

sourcePatterns.description: Script element created and injected with decoded source — dynamic script tag injection
sourcePatterns.note: Creating a script element and setting its source or content to a decoded value (atob, decodeURI, unescape, fromCharCode) is dynamic script injection with obfuscated payload. The actual code is decoded from an encoded form at runtime.
