sourcePatterns.description: Fake Apple App Store page impersonating app listing — malicious iOS profile/PWA delivery
sourcePatterns.note: Fake Apple App Store pages with app listings, ratings, and GET/Download buttons trick iOS users into installing malicious profiles, PWAs, or enterprise-signed apps outside the real App Store.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake iOS Smart App Banner prompting app install — impersonates native iOS UI element
sourcePatterns.note: Fake Smart App Banners using apple-itunes-app meta tags trick iOS users into thinking there's a native app to install. The banner links to a phishing PWA or sideloaded enterprise app instead of the real App Store.

sourcePatterns.description: SMS or tel: scheme URI used in page links — triggers phone call or text from browser
sourcePatterns.note: SMS URIs with pre-filled body text (sms:+1234?body=...) can auto-compose messages on the victim's phone. Phishing pages use these to make victims send attacker-controlled text to premium numbers or other victims.

sourcePatterns.description: WhatsApp share link with pre-filled phishing message — social engineering amplification
sourcePatterns.note: WhatsApp share links (wa.me/send?text=...) with pre-filled URLs let phishing pages amplify attacks by making victims share phishing links with their contacts. The shared link comes from a trusted contact.

sourcePatterns.description: Telegram share link with pre-filled phishing URL — social engineering amplification
sourcePatterns.note: Telegram share links (t.me/share/url?url=...) with pre-filled phishing URLs amplify campaigns by making victims share malicious links within their Telegram contacts.

sourcePatterns.description: Mobile carrier billing or account verification form — carrier impersonation phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Mobile carrier billing/account verification pages that request payment updates are used in carrier impersonation phishing targeting T-Mobile, Verizon, AT&T, and other providers.

sourcePatterns.description: Custom 'Add to Home Screen' instruction overlay for PWA install — mobile app phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Custom overlay instructions telling users how to 'Add to Home Screen' guide victims through installing a phishing PWA as an app. The instructions are tailored to iOS (share button → Add to Home Screen) or Android (menu → Install App).

sourcePatterns.description: Mobile status bar elements rendered in HTML — spoofing native device UI
sourcePatterns.note: Rendering a fake mobile status bar (signal bars, battery, time, 4G/5G) in HTML positioned at the top of the page makes the phishing page look like it's running inside a native app rather than a browser.

sourcePatterns.description: Haptic/vibration feedback simulating authentication confirmation — fake success feeling
sourcePatterns.note: Using the Vibration API to provide haptic feedback when credentials are 'verified' creates a convincing physical confirmation feeling on the victim's phone. The vibration makes the fake authentication feel real.

sourcePatterns.description: Double login prompt — 'incorrect password' error after first submission forcing second credential entry
sourcePatterns.note: AiTM double credential prompt: shows error to collect password twice. Requires form submission context — generic error messages alone are too broad.

sourcePatterns.description: Countdown timer creating urgency — account action expires in X minutes/hours pattern
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript countdown timers showing 'X minutes remaining' or 'expires in...' create artificial urgency on phishing pages. The timer pressures victims to enter credentials quickly before 'time runs out'. [Observation-level: Countdown timer — sales pages, event countdowns, session timeouts]

sourcePatterns.description: Mailto link to a suspicious email address on a login page — fake support contact for social engineering
sourcePatterns.note: A mailto link to a free email provider (Gmail, Yahoo, Hotmail) on a login page is suspicious. Legitimate enterprise services use their own domain for support contacts, not free email accounts.

sourcePatterns.description: Customs duty or import fee payment form — fake customs clearance requiring credit card payment
sourcePatterns.note: Customs duty/import fee payment forms are one of the most common delivery scam pages. The page claims a package is held in customs and requires payment of a small fee ($1-$5) to release it. The fee is a pretext to collect credit card details.

sourcePatterns.description: Tariff or de minimis duty payment scam — exploits 2025 tariff rule confusion
sourcePatterns.note: Post-August 2025 tariff scams exploit consumer confusion about new US de minimis tariff rules on imports under $800. Scammers claim a tariff must be paid before delivery. The FCC specifically warned about this emerging scam vector.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake tracking number displayed alongside payment request — courier impersonation with tracking theater
sourcePatterns.note: Displaying a fake tracking number (that looks real but isn't) alongside a payment or action request is the core courier phishing pattern. The tracking number adds perceived legitimacy but the fee/verify request is the scam.

sourcePatterns.description: Package held/on hold/pending urgency text — delivery status pressure for immediate action
sourcePatterns.note: Text claiming a package is 'on hold', 'pending', 'delayed', or 'awaiting action' combined with a required action (pay, verify, update) creates urgency. This is the fundamental social engineering pattern of ALL delivery phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Delivery scheduling or rescheduling fee form — fake redelivery charge to collect payment details
sourcePatterns.note: Delivery scheduling/rescheduling fee forms claim a small charge ($0.99-$3.99) is required to reschedule a missed delivery. Real courier services don't charge rescheduling fees via SMS/email links. The fee is a pretext for card harvesting.

sourcePatterns.description: SMS-style short delivery notification text on landing page — smishing landing page indicator
sourcePatterns.note: Short notification-style text about a delivery status (delivered, attempted, held, delayed, missed) is the landing page for an SMS phishing link. The text matches what was in the original smishing SMS that brought the victim to this page.

sourcePatterns.description: Postage due or insufficient postage payment page — fee collection for undelivered mail
sourcePatterns.note: Postage due/insufficient postage payment pages claim additional shipping fees must be paid to receive a package. These are common in USPS, Royal Mail, and La Poste impersonation phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake courier app download prompt — directs users to install malicious tracking app
sourcePatterns.note: Fake courier app download prompts impersonate DHL, FedEx, USPS, etc. and direct users to install malicious APKs or fake apps. The 'tracking app' is actually malware that can intercept SMS (for OTP theft), access contacts, and steal banking credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Unpaid toll balance notification with late fee threat — the core toll phishing social engineering text
sourcePatterns.note: The exact text pattern of toll phishing: 'unpaid toll balance' + threat of $25-$50 late fee/penalty + 'pay now to avoid'. This is the social engineering text on every Smishing Triad toll landing page. 60,000+ FBI complaints about this exact pattern.

sourcePatterns.description: Toll account suspension or hold warning — threatens account action if payment not made
sourcePatterns.note: Threatening toll account suspension/hold if payment isn't made is the escalation tactic used in toll phishing. Real toll agencies send physical mail for collections, not SMS threats of account suspension.

sourcePatterns.description: Toll violation or invoice number displayed — fake reference number adding perceived legitimacy
sourcePatterns.note: Displaying a fake violation/invoice number (like 'Violation #TX-89234' or 'Invoice: 7829341') alongside a toll balance adds perceived legitimacy. The number is generated per-visit and doesn't correspond to any real toll record.

sourcePatterns.description: Vehicle registration or DMV action threatened for unpaid tolls — escalation scare tactic
sourcePatterns.note: Threatening DMV action, vehicle registration holds, or license plate suspension for unpaid tolls is the highest-pressure escalation in toll phishing. Real toll agencies follow lengthy legal processes, not instant SMS threats.

sourcePatterns.description: Collections agency or legal action threatened for toll balance — extreme pressure escalation
sourcePatterns.note: Threatening collections agency referral, legal action, or credit score impact for unpaid tolls is extreme-pressure toll phishing. Real toll debt follows months of physical mail notices before any collections referral.

sourcePatterns.description: Hidden preheader text claiming email was scanned/verified — ZeroFont fake security assurance
sourcePatterns.note: Hidden text claiming 'Scanned and secured by Advanced Threat Protection' or similar fake security assurances appears in email previews, making the message look legitimate. SANS ISC documented this as a 'new spin' on ZeroFont in September 2023, still active.

sourcePatterns.description: Shared document viewing prompt requiring sign-in — document lure phishing landing page
sourcePatterns.note: Pages claiming 'Someone shared a document with you' followed by a sign-in prompt are the most common enterprise phishing lure. The victim thinks they need to authenticate to view a shared file, but the login form harvests their credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Blurred or partially visible document preview behind login overlay — document lure visual trick
sourcePatterns.note: A blurred or partially transparent document preview behind a login overlay is the visual trick that makes shared document phishing convincing. The victim can see enough of the document to believe it's real, but must 'sign in' to view it clearly.

sourcePatterns.description: Voicemail notification with listen/play button requiring sign-in — voicemail phishing landing page
sourcePatterns.note: Voicemail notification pages with play/listen buttons that require sign-in are a top enterprise phishing lure. Microsoft voicemail notifications are frequently impersonated, with the login form harvesting M365 credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake audio player UI for voicemail — visual audio waveform or player controls on phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: A fake audio player UI showing waveform, play button, progress bar, and duration (e.g., '0:45') for a voicemail message is the visual component of voicemail phishing. The player doesn't actually play audio — it's just HTML/CSS that gates a login form.

sourcePatterns.description: Subscription auto-renewal charge notification — payment method update or cancellation phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Subscription auto-renewal alerts claiming 'Your subscription has been renewed for $XXX' with options to cancel or update payment are a top consumer phishing lure. Norton, McAfee, Geek Squad, and streaming services are commonly impersonated.

sourcePatterns.description: Job offer requiring upfront payment or equipment deposit — advance fee employment scam
sourcePatterns.note: Job offers requiring upfront payments for training, equipment, background checks, or registration are advance fee employment scams. Legitimate employers never charge employees to start working.

sourcePatterns.description: ClickFix Run dialog instructions — tells user to press Win+R and paste command
sourcePatterns.note: ClickFix instructions telling user to press Win+R, paste, and press Enter. No legitimate CAPTCHA ever asks a user to open the Run dialog.

sourcePatterns.description: ClickFix fake verification steps — numbered steps combining CAPTCHA with command execution
sourcePatterns.note: ClickFix uses numbered steps progressing from verification language to command execution language. This step progression is unique to ClickFix.

sourcePatterns.description: Callback phishing — subscription/invoice with phone number to call for cancellation
sourcePatterns.note: Callback phishing shows fake subscription/invoice and tells victim to CALL a phone number to cancel. Phone connects to scammers who install remote access software. CrowdStrike reported 442% vishing growth.

sourcePatterns.description: Large dollar amount with phone number as only action — callback scam indicator
sourcePatterns.note: Large dollar amount ($100-$9999) with phone number and no online cancellation is the callback phishing layout.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake MFA enrollment QR code — impersonates authenticator app setup
sourcePatterns.note: Fake MFA enrollment QR codes. Nearly 1 in 4 campaigns use QR codes as MFA prompts per Hoxhunt.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake job offer collecting sensitive PII — recruitment phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Fake job offers collecting SSN, DOB, bank details. Legitimate apps never ask for SSN in initial forms.

sourcePatterns.description: HR portal impersonation — performance review, salary, benefits, W-2 phishing
sourcePatterns.note: HR portal impersonation pages are among the top AI-generated lure categories in 2025-2026.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake meeting invite with login requirement — calendar-based phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Fake meeting invites requiring login to 'join'. Calendar phishing is an emerging bypass for email security.

sourcePatterns.description: Fake DocuSign/electronic signature page requiring login — document signing phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Fake DocuSign pages requiring login to 'view' or 'sign' documents. DocuSign is one of the most impersonated brands in ClickFix campaigns.

sourcePatterns.description: Blurred or obscured document preview image with login form overlay — document lure credential harvest
sourcePatterns.note: A blurred or semi-transparent document image behind a login form is the #1 document lure layout. The 'document' is just an image with CSS blur/opacity applied, and a credential modal sits on top. No legitimate document-sharing service blurs a real document behind a login wall.

sourcePatterns.description: View/Open/Access Document button on a page with password input — document lure leading to credential form
sourcePatterns.note: A 'View Document' or 'Open File' button/heading on the same page as a password input field is a document lure phishing layout. Legitimate file sharing shows the document directly or uses the hosting service's own login, not a generic password prompt on the same page.

sourcePatterns.description: Generic document sharing notification with login requirement — shared file credential phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Generic 'X has shared a document with you' notification text followed by a 'sign in to view' prompt is the standard document lure layout. Legitimate sharing notifications link to the hosting service's own authenticated view, not a generic login prompt.

sourcePatterns.description: Invoice/remittance/purchase order document with credential or payment form — BEC document lure
sourcePatterns.note: Invoice/remittance/PO/RFQ document text combined with a login prompt or password field on the same page is BEC document lure phishing. Real invoices are attached as PDFs or viewed in accounting software — they don't require signing in on the same page.

sourcePatterns.description: Document preview as static background image with centered login modal — image-based document lure
sourcePatterns.note: A static document/invoice/PDF image as background with a positioned login modal on top is the visual layout of image-based document lures. The 'document' is a screenshot or placeholder image, not real interactive content. The login modal steals credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Secure message or encrypted email notification with login — secure delivery impersonation phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Fake 'secure message' or 'encrypted email' notifications that require signing in to view the message are a popular phishing lure. They impersonate services like Zix, Proofpoint Encryption, or Microsoft 365 Message Encryption. The login form harvests credentials.

sourcePatterns.description: Fax or voicemail notification requiring login — legacy communication lure for credential harvest
sourcePatterns.note: Fake fax or voicemail notifications requiring login to 'listen' or 'view' the message are legacy communication lures. They impersonate services like eFax, RingCentral, or Microsoft 365 voicemail. The login form is the actual phishing payload.

sourcePatterns.description: Document metadata (page count, file size) displayed as preview lure — fake document information theater
sourcePatterns.note: Displaying document metadata (page count, file size, file type) alongside a View/Open/Download button that leads to a login form is document information theater. Real document sharing services show this metadata WITH the document, not instead of it behind a login wall.

sourcePatterns.description: Sender name and email displayed in document sharing notification with login — personalized document lure
sourcePatterns.note: Showing the sender's name and email address in a document sharing notification adds perceived legitimacy (the victim recognizes the sender). Combined with a View Document button and a login form, this is a personalized document lure. The sender info is often extracted from the victim's email.

sourcePatterns.description: Login/Sign In button text combined with document/file access — compound action button phishing
sourcePatterns.note: Button text like Login To View File — definitive phishing language. Kept regex for the phrase matching.

sourcePatterns.description: Radio button action choices on a credential page — fake options that all submit the same form
sourcePatterns.note: Two or more radio buttons offering different file actions (View Online, Download File, Send File) on a page with a credential form is action theater. The radio buttons create an illusion of choice but all options submit the same credential-harvesting form. Real file services don't mix action selection with login.

sourcePatterns.description: Formal authoritative language about access rights — legal-sounding text suppressing skepticism on phishing pages
sourcePatterns.note: Formal, legal-sounding language about access rights ('rightful recipient', 'unauthorized access is prohibited', 'protected file') combined with a login form creates false authority. This language discourages the victim from questioning the page's legitimacy. Real login pages don't lecture about unauthorized access.

sourcePatterns.description: Netflix-specific signin language with credential prompts
sourcePatterns.note: This exact phrasing differs from legitimate Netflix login flow and indicates impersonation

sourcePatterns.description: Delivery exception with address verification failure and update prompt
sourcePatterns.note: Targets logistics delivery scams across DPD, UPS, FedEx campaigns using address verification social engineering

sourcePatterns.description: Large blocks of invisible text for SEO stuffing on phishing pages
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing pages stuff invisible SEO text to manipulate search rankings or evade content-based filters. The text contains credential-related keywords but is hidden from view.

sourcePatterns.description: Blurred background with login overlay — simulates document preview requiring authentication
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing pages show a blurred document or preview behind a login overlay, tricking victims into thinking they need to authenticate to view content.

sourcePatterns.description: Shows 'incorrect password' error after first submission to harvest multiple password attempts
sourcePatterns.note: Classic phishkit technique: show 'incorrect password' after first submission to harvest multiple password attempts, then redirect to the real site. The attempt counter variable is the distinctive signal.

sourcePatterns.description: QR code generated dynamically on phishing page to redirect victims to mobile
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing pages generating QR codes alongside credential fields push victims to less-protected mobile devices. Low-moderate weight.

sourcePatterns.description: DocuSign-style envelope/document review language combined with credential form
sourcePatterns.note: DocuSign-impersonation phishing with credential forms is extremely common. Real DocuSign pages don't ask for email passwords.

sourcePatterns.description: Warning about never sharing recovery phrase - common trust-building technique on crypto phishing pages
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing pages often include legitimate-sounding warnings about recovery phrases to appear trustworthy

sourcePatterns.description: Fake Facebook copyright enforcement notice threatening account suspension
sourcePatterns.note: Matches fake AI copyright enforcement warnings with time pressure - legitimate Meta notices don't use this exact phrasing pattern

sourcePatterns.description: Fake Meta/Facebook account appeal code with suspicious format
sourcePatterns.note: Detects fake appeal codes with specific format patterns used in Meta impersonation scams - real Meta doesn't use this code structure

sourcePatterns.description: Urgent account suspension warning with appeal code mechanism
sourcePatterns.note: Combines urgency tactics (suspension + time limit) with appeal code harvesting mechanism. Pattern specific enough to avoid legitimate account recovery pages.

sourcePatterns.description: All-numeric domain pattern often used for phishing
Western brand impersonation on Chinese domains
Random subdomain on GoDaddy website builder indicating potential phishing
Discord official page title on non-Discord domain

sourcePatterns.description: Detects page titles claiming to be 'Official Apple' support/contact pages on non-Apple domains
Detects Unicode homoglyph characters (mathematical script, accented Latin) used in title or meta tags to spell crypto/financial brand names while evading text matching — e.g. G𝓮miñi for Gemini
Detects Microsoft account sign-in page title impersonation used by credential harvesting phishkits
Detects Cloudflare challenge impersonation pages with common misspelling 'Challange' in title or body
Detects pages using Dropbox sign-in page title patterns with credential fields, indicating brand impersonation when served from non-Dropbox domains
Detects DocuSign login page title impersonation pattern in HTML title or visible text
Page title contains Instagram login/signin branding on a non-Instagram domain
Page title containing 'Log in' or 'Log into' with 'Facebook' on non-Facebook domain
Page title containing 'Outlook 365 Login' — used by phishing kits impersonating Microsoft Outlook; real Microsoft pages never use this exact title
Generic Amazon project title commonly used on Amazon clone/impersonation sites
sourcePatterns.note: Amateur Amazon impersonation sites often use generic titles like 'Amazon Project' instead of legitimate Amazon page titles

sourcePatterns.description: Dynamic document.title manipulation to impersonate a major brand login page
sourcePatterns.note: Setting document.title dynamically to include both a login prompt and a major brand name is a phishing indicator. Legitimate login pages set their title server-side.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating Zoom meeting or sign-in page
sourcePatterns.note: Zoom meeting join and login page title impersonation on non-Zoom domains. Zoom phishing is prevalent for business credential theft.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating WeTransfer file download page
sourcePatterns.note: WeTransfer title impersonation is used in file-sharing phishing campaigns that trick users into entering credentials to 'download' shared files.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating DHL tracking or delivery page
sourcePatterns.note: DHL tracking page impersonation is a top logistics phishing vector. Fake delivery notification pages harvest personal and payment information.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating USPS tracking or redelivery page
sourcePatterns.note: USPS impersonation is one of the most common logistics phishing campaigns, especially targeting US users with fake redelivery fee pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating FedEx tracking or delivery page
sourcePatterns.note: FedEx tracking page impersonation targets users with fake delivery notifications that harvest personal or payment information.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title impersonating LinkedIn login or notification page
sourcePatterns.note: LinkedIn login impersonation targets business professionals. Credential theft from LinkedIn accounts enables BEC and social engineering attacks.

sourcePatterns.description: Page title is just a brand name in lowercase with no context — lazy phishing kit title
sourcePatterns.note: Title tag containing only a brand name — phishing pages use bare brand names as titles.

sourcePatterns.description: Generic account verification or security alert title — common phishing lure
sourcePatterns.note: Generic urgency titles like 'Account Verification' or 'Unusual Sign-in Activity' without brand names. Moderate weight because some legitimate services use similar titles.

sourcePatterns.description: Document sharing lure in page title — impersonates SharePoint/OneDrive/Google Drive
sourcePatterns.note: Document sharing lures in the page title are extremely common in phishing. Moderate weight — legitimate share notifications have similar titles but load on brand domains.

sourcePatterns.description: Voicemail notification lure in page title — credential harvesting technique
sourcePatterns.note: Voicemail notification phishing is a major trend in 2025. Pages claiming to have voicemails in their title that also require login are almost always phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: Invoice or payment notification lure in page title
sourcePatterns.note: Invoice and payment-themed titles are common phishing lures, especially those impersonating DocuSign or similar services.

sourcePatterns.description: Fax or scanned document lure in page title
sourcePatterns.note: Fax document phishing lures are a classic attack vector targeting enterprise users. Nearly no legitimate service uses 'New Fax' as a web page title in 2025.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft branding combined with login/account keywords in page title or main headers on non-Microsoft domain
sourcePatterns.note: Requires Microsoft brand AND login/account context in title to reduce FP on legitimate tech sites; bounded quantifiers for performance

sourcePatterns.description: Page title claiming to be official crypto wallet login with security messaging
sourcePatterns.note: Matches titles impersonating crypto wallet login pages with official/secure language

Detects hostnames containing 'myarv' prefix pattern impersonating Arvest Bank's myarvest.com portal on non-Arvest domains

IPFS-hosted pages with password fields indicating credential harvesting

Detects 'my' prefix hostnames on high-risk TLDs (.icu, .top, .xyz, .sbs) commonly used for banking portal phishing
Detects random alphanumeric prefix followed by brand-related keyword in subdomain, indicating wildcard DNS abuse on compromised domain
URL query parameter selects brand name for phishkit template rendering (e.g. ?d=swedbank, ?brand=paypal)

Detects domains using brand-job/career/hiring pattern with fake meeting or reference IDs commonly used in employment scam phishkits
Constant Contact landing page URL pattern commonly abused for phishing
Detects Google Sites pages where the URL path contains a third-party domain (attacker's Workspace domain) — indicates abuse of Google Sites for brand impersonation
Detects base64-encoded email addresses in URL query parameters, common in targeted phishing campaigns
URL path contains a base64-encoded string that likely encodes an email address (contains @ when decoded), indicating per-victim phishing targeting
Detects domains combining login/service keywords on .cloud TLD with randomized short path identifiers typical of phishing campaign infrastructure
USPS phishkit URL pattern with /com/ path segment and index-style HTML files, commonly seen on throwaway domains
Page on duckdns.org free DDNS contains an HTML form — extremely high phishing correlation

sourcePatterns.description: Venmo-branded PHP logging endpoint in URL structure
sourcePatterns.note: Detects Venmo-specific credential harvesting PHP endpoints in HTML attributes

sourcePatterns.description: Venmo-branded URL with PHP credential harvesting endpoint and randomized session path
sourcePatterns.note: Specific to Venmo phishkits using PHP backends with characteristic naming conventions - legitimate Venmo never uses PHP endpoints

sourcePatterns.description: Base64-encoded email address detected in URL parameter within page links
sourcePatterns.note: Base64-encoded values in URL parameters named email/user/login/id/token indicate personalized phishing where the victim's email is encoded in the URL for tracking or form pre-fill.

sourcePatterns.description: Form action URL pointing to a suspicious TLD domain — exfiltration to cheap/free domains
sourcePatterns.note: Form actions pointing to domains on suspicious TLDs (.top, .xyz, .buzz, .live, .icu, .tk, etc.) indicate credential exfiltration to cheap/free domain infrastructure. These TLDs are disproportionately used for phishing.

sourcePatterns.description: JavaScript redirect to suspicious TLD — phishing redirect chain endpoint
sourcePatterns.note: JavaScript redirects to suspicious TLDs indicate the page is part of a phishing redirect chain that bounces through multiple domains.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple sequential redirects in JavaScript — phishing redirect chain
sourcePatterns.note: Two or more redirect assignments in the same script indicate a redirect chain. Phishing kits bounce through multiple intermediary pages before landing on the final credential harvesting page.

sourcePatterns.description: URL shortener used in JavaScript redirect — obscured phishing destination
sourcePatterns.note: Redirecting through URL shorteners in JavaScript hides the final phishing destination from both users and URL-based detection. No legitimate login page redirects through shortener services.

sourcePatterns.description: DuckDNS dynamic DNS domain in page links — free DNS commonly abused for phishing
sourcePatterns.note: DuckDNS is a free dynamic DNS service heavily abused for phishing infrastructure. The .duckdns.org subdomain indicates attacker-controlled dynamic DNS infrastructure.

sourcePatterns.description: Google open redirect URLs used in page source — abuses Google's trusted domain for redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Using Google's open redirect endpoints (google.com/url?q=, google.com/amp/s/) to bounce through Google's domain makes phishing links appear to come from Google. This abuses Google's trusted reputation.

sourcePatterns.description: Microsoft open redirect URLs used in page source — abuses Microsoft's trusted domain
sourcePatterns.note: Abusing Microsoft's open redirectors to bounce phishing links through Microsoft's trusted domain. Common in BEC campaigns targeting Office 365 users.

sourcePatterns.description: Custom app deep link schemes in page source — potential app-based phishing redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Custom URI schemes for messaging apps (whatsapp://, telegram://, signal://) in page links can deep-link into apps with pre-filled malicious content, contact additions, or group invites used in social engineering chains.

sourcePatterns.description: Chinese .TOP domain in page source links — Smishing Triad infrastructure indicator
sourcePatterns.note: The Smishing Triad predominantly uses .TOP domains for their toll road, delivery, and payment phishing campaigns. Links to .top domains combined with logistics/toll/payment keywords are characteristic of this Chinese smishing group.

sourcePatterns.description: Link-in-bio service used as phishing redirect — abuses social media link tools
sourcePatterns.note: Link-in-bio services (Linktree, Beacons, Stan, Bio.link) are abused as phishing redirectors because their domains are trusted and rarely blocked. The phishing link hides behind a legitimate social media link tool.

sourcePatterns.description: Footer navigation links pointing to # or javascript:void — phishing page with non-functional navigation
sourcePatterns.note: Two or more footer/navigation links pointing to # or javascript:void(0) indicates a phishing page with decorative non-functional links. Phishers copy the visual layout of legitimate sites but don't bother making footer links work.

sourcePatterns.description: Base href tag pointing to a different domain — makes relative URLs resolve to attacker's server
sourcePatterns.note: A <base href> tag pointing to an external domain makes ALL relative URLs on the page resolve to that domain instead of the current one. Phishers use this to load assets from a different server while hosting the phishing page elsewhere.

sourcePatterns.description: Page with empty, blank, or extremely generic title — phishing pages often lack proper titles
sourcePatterns.note: Phishing pages often have generic, blank, or single-word titles like 'Document', 'Login', 'Verify', 'Untitled'. Legitimate login pages always have branded titles (e.g., 'Sign in to Microsoft'). A generic title on a credential page is suspicious.

sourcePatterns.description: Page resources loaded from an IP address rather than a domain name — suspicious hosting indicator
sourcePatterns.note: Loading page resources (scripts, images, form actions) from raw IP addresses instead of domain names is a hosting indicator. Legitimate sites use domain names; phishing infrastructure often uses IP addresses to avoid domain takedowns.

sourcePatterns.description: Login page with no navigation menu or site links — isolated credential form with no site context
sourcePatterns.note: Simplified from 5KB wildcard scan to content checks. Detects minimal login-only pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Form action URL using a cheap/suspicious TLD — .xyz, .top, .tk, .ml, .ga, .cf, .gq hosting
sourcePatterns.note: Form actions posting to cheap or commonly-abused TLDs (.xyz, .top, .tk, .ml, .ga, .cf, .gq, .icu, etc.) are strong phishing indicators. These TLDs are disproportionately used for phishing due to low cost or free registration.

sourcePatterns.description: Multiple anchor tags with empty href — decorative non-functional links on phishing page
sourcePatterns.note: Three or more anchor tags with empty href attributes on the same page are decorative links that go nowhere. Phishing pages include visual link text (Terms, Privacy, Help) but with empty hrefs because the phisher didn't set up those pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Meta refresh tag with delayed redirect — auto-redirect to phishing page or away from evidence
sourcePatterns.note: A meta refresh tag with a URL redirect can auto-redirect to a phishing page from a compromised/trusted site, or redirect away from the phishing page after credential capture to destroy evidence.

sourcePatterns.description: Page where all hyperlinks point to external domains — no internal navigation structure
sourcePatterns.note: A page where all links are absolute URLs to external domains (no relative internal links) indicates a page without internal site structure. Phishing pages link out to the real brand site for Terms/Privacy but have no internal pages.

sourcePatterns.description: Redirect through trusted intermediate service before phishing landing — Tycoon's redirect laundering
sourcePatterns.note: Tycoon chains redirects through trusted services (Firebase, Azure Blob, Wix, TikTok, Google Docs/Sites) before landing on the phishing page. These trusted intermediate domains lend legitimacy to the redirect chain and bypass URL reputation checks.

sourcePatterns.description: Domain using .xin TLD in page resources — Smishing Triad toll phishing infrastructure indicator
sourcePatterns.note: The .xin TLD (Chinese for 'new/faith', managed by Elegant Leader Limited in Hong Kong) is specifically used by Smishing Triad for toll phishing domains. Resecurity identified thousands of toll phishing domains registered under .xin.

sourcePatterns.description: Google Translate URL rewrite hiding phishing destination — trusted domain redirect
sourcePatterns.note: Google Translate URL rewrite makes phishing load through Google's domain. 48% of phishing links use URL redirection.

sourcePatterns.description: Google AMP cache URL hiding phishing page — CDN-cached redirect abuse
sourcePatterns.note: Google AMP cache serves phishing pages through Google's CDN, inheriting Google's domain reputation.

sourcePatterns.description: Open redirect through trusted service — redirect parameter abuse on major platforms
sourcePatterns.note: Open redirect parameters on trusted services bounce victims from a trusted domain to the phishing page.

sourcePatterns.description: Page loads resources from multiple unrelated domains — phishing kit with scattered asset hosting
sourcePatterns.note: Loading legitimate CDN resources (Google Fonts, cdnjs) on a page that posts to a PHP credential collector or Telegram/Discord webhook indicates a phishing kit using legitimate CDNs for visual polish while exfiltrating through attacker infrastructure.

sourcePatterns.description: Logistics brand subdomain on suspicious TLD
sourcePatterns.note: Detects logistics brands hosted on suspicious TLDs commonly used for delivery scams

networkPatterns.description: POST body contains well-known session cookie name=value pairs being exfiltrated
networkPatterns.note: Phishkits exfiltrate stolen session cookies in POST bodies. These session cookie names belong to known frameworks/services (PHP, Java, .NET, Node.js, Laravel, Microsoft). Seeing them in a POST body to a cross-origin destination is definitive session theft.

networkPatterns.description: POST body field name explicitly labeled as cookie data being exfiltrated
networkPatterns.note: Phishkits label exfiltrated cookie data with descriptive field names. No legitimate application uses field names like stolen_cookie or victim_cookie.

networkPatterns.description: sendBeacon transmitting cookie-shaped data on page unload — session theft
networkPatterns.note: sendBeacon fires on page unload, making it ideal for last-chance cookie exfil that survives tab close. Session cookies in beacon payloads are definitive theft signals.

networkPatterns.description: POST body contains what appears to be a full document.cookie string dump
networkPatterns.note: Phishkits dump the full document.cookie string into a POST body field. The pattern matches a field named cookie/cookies containing multiple semicolon-separated key=value pairs — the signature shape of a cookie dump.

networkPatterns.description: POST body contains Microsoft ESTSAUTH/ESTSAUTHPERSIST token — AiTM session hijack
networkPatterns.note: ESTSAUTH and ESTSAUTHPERSIST are Microsoft authentication cookies used by Azure AD/Entra ID. Finding these in an outbound POST body is near-definitive AiTM session hijacking (Evilginx, Tycoon2FA, EvilProxy). These tokens start with 0. or eyJ (JWT).

networkPatterns.description: POST to attacker drop server endpoint with cookie payload
networkPatterns.note: Phishkits POST stolen cookies to drop server endpoints with descriptive paths. Legitimate APIs don't use endpoint paths containing words like collector, drop, exfil, or steal combined with cookie/session/token.

networkPatterns.description: POST to PHP endpoint with phishkit-convention filename (send, grab, log, next, rezult, post)
networkPatterns.note: Matches the destination URL of outbound requests, not page source. These PHP filenames are strong phishkit conventions. Legitimate WordPress admin-ajax.php and wp-login.php are excluded by pattern specificity.

networkPatterns.description: POST body contains phishkit-convention field names (victim, rezult, passw, usr_pass)
networkPatterns.note: Matches extracted field name keys from POST body. These names are phishkit conventions never used by legitimate applications. The passw pattern excludes 'password' (legitimate).

networkPatterns.description: POST to formspree.io — legitimate form backend commonly abused by phishkits
networkPatterns.note: Formspree is a legitimate form backend but commonly abused by phishkits. This fires only when a POST actually goes to formspree — paired with other page signals, this is strong evidence.

networkPatterns.description: POST body contains base64-encoded string alongside multiple credential field names
networkPatterns.note: Phishkits sometimes base64-encode the entire credential payload before exfiltrating. The pattern requires at least 2 credential-like fields AND a long base64 string.

networkPatterns.description: POST to getform.io — form backend abused for credential exfiltration
networkPatterns.note: getform.io is a form endpoint service frequently abused by phishkits for credential collection.

networkPatterns.description: SMTP.js library loaded for email-based credential exfiltration
networkPatterns.note: SMTP.js is a client-side email library commonly used by phishkits to exfiltrate stolen credentials - no legitimate login page uses this

networkPatterns.description: POST request to SMTP.js API for email-based credential exfiltration
networkPatterns.note: Catches actual credential exfiltration via SMTP.js email service. No legitimate business application would use SMTP.js for sensitive data transmission.

networkPatterns.description: POST to Telegram Bot API sendMessage endpoint — credential exfiltration in progress
networkPatterns.note: This is the actual exfil POST happening, not just the URL in source code. Near-definitive signal — no legitimate login page POSTs to Telegram.

networkPatterns.description: POST to Discord webhook endpoint — credential exfiltration in progress
networkPatterns.note: Discord webhooks with full tokens in the URL are definitive phishkit exfil. Legitimate Discord integrations use OAuth, not raw webhook URLs on login pages.

networkPatterns.description: POST to SendGrid mail API — client-side email exfiltration of stolen credentials
networkPatterns.note: Phishkits use SendGrid to email stolen credentials to the attacker. Legitimate pages don't call SendGrid's mail API from client-side JavaScript.

networkPatterns.description: POST to Slack incoming webhook — credential exfiltration channel
networkPatterns.note: Slack incoming webhooks with full token path are not called from legitimate login pages. Phishkits use them to post stolen credentials to attacker Slack channels.

networkPatterns.description: sendBeacon transmitting credential-shaped data on page unload
networkPatterns.note: sendBeacon fires on page unload, ideal for last-chance credential exfil that survives tab close. Legitimate sites use beacons for analytics payloads, not credential data.

networkPatterns.description: POST to Mailgun API — client-side email exfiltration of stolen credentials
networkPatterns.note: Mailgun's send API called from client-side code is a definitive phishkit signal. Legitimate integrations call Mailgun server-side.

metaRules.description: Two or more AITM (adversary-in-the-middle) proxy signals on the same page — confirms an active session hijacking phishkit, not just a coincidental pattern match
metaRules.note: AITM kits are the most dangerous phishing category — they steal session tokens, not just passwords. Two AITM signals together is near-definitive.

metaRules.description: Telegram exfiltration code combined with brand impersonation — confirmed credential harvesting phishkit exfiltrating to Telegram
metaRules.note: Telegram exfil alone scores 0.35-0.50. Brand impersonation alone scores 0.25-0.45. Together they are definitive phishing.

metaRules.description: Multi-step authentication flow with external form submission or CAPTCHA gating — confirms a credential harvesting phishkit, not a legitimate multi-step login
metaRules.note: Multi-step auth is common on legitimate sites (score 0.25-0.30). Adding external form post eliminates almost all legitimate cases.

metaRules.description: Free hosting platform + brand impersonation + password form — classic phishing deployment on free infrastructure
metaRules.note: Hosting signals are low weight (0.15-0.25) because the platforms are legitimate. Brand + password form confirms malicious intent.

metaRules.description: ClickFix phishkit signature combined with social engineering lure — confirmed malware delivery via clipboard injection
metaRules.note: ClickFix clipboard injection (0.50) + run dialog instructions (0.40) are both high individually but the combination is 100% malicious.

metaRules.description: Document/shared-file social engineering lure combined with brand impersonation and a login form — classic 'view this document' phishing flow
metaRules.note: Document lure patterns score 0.20-0.35 individually. With a login form they become definitive credential phishing.

metaRules.description: Domain < 7 days old with a password form posting to an external endpoint — nearly definitive credential phishing
Page title impersonates a known brand AND page has a password form — classic credential phishing page
Page title impersonates a known brand AND form submits to external domain — credential exfiltration
Screenshot matches a known brand login page AND domain is < 30 days old — high-confidence brand impersonation phishing
DOM structure matches a known phishkit template AND page has a form posting externally — confirmed phishkit deployment
Page uses bot evasion techniques AND has credential harvesting patterns — phishkit with anti-analysis
Page has obfuscated JavaScript AND impersonates a known brand — phishkit hiding its payload
Page uses CDN/CAPTCHA gating AND has a password form — scanner shield protecting a credential harvester
IDN homograph domain impersonating a brand AND page has a password form — near-certain credential phishing via Unicode domain spoofing
IDN homograph brand impersonation on a domain less than 30 days old — freshly registered Unicode lookalike is almost exclusively phishing
Mixed-script IDN domain (no brand match) combined with credential harvesting source patterns — the script mixing plus harvesting code confirms malicious intent
Both WHOIS and CT fired for the same new domain — replace with a single calibrated age signal instead of double-counting
Brand detected via typosquat AND subdomain AND path — these are all detecting the same brand impersonation, cap at 0.55
Bot evasion signals on a free subdomain host are common on legitimate sites (Cloudflare challenges, etc.) — reduce combined weight
Social engineering language detected but no form on the page — likely a legitimate urgency message, not a phishing page

Both IDN homograph and ASCII homoglyph signals fired for the same hostname — they detect the same brand impersonation via different normalization paths. Replace with a single calibrated weight to prevent double-counting.
